coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of carbon dots intercalated MXene hybrids via laser treatment as oil-based additives for synergistic lubrication
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yuhong
Xue, Shenghua
Wang, Shiyuan
Chen, Xin
Liu, Shujuan
Ye, Qian
Zhou, Feng
Liu, Weimin
coredata.dc:description: As an emerging two-dimensional material, MXene exhibits excellent anti-friction and anti-wear properties in lubrication systems. However, as an oil-based lubricant additive, their stable dispersion in base oil still faces great challenges. Optimizing the microstructure has been proved to be an effective strategy to improve the stable dispersion of MXene in base oil. In this work, catechol groups functionalized carbon dots are synthesized successfully by pulsed laser irradiation of tannic acid in acetone, and then they are grafted onto MXene surface to obtain MXene@CDs hybrids via chemical bond between catechol groups of carbon dots and Ti–OH of MXene surface. Carbon dots not only increase the layer spacing of MXene nanosheets, reduce self-weight stacking, but also make it easier to slide between layers. When used as oil based lubricating additives, MXene@CDs can reduce the coefficient of friction of PAO 10 from 0.58 to 0.1, and the wear volume is reduced by 91.3%. Due to the shear-induced and self-healing mechanism, the special intercalation structure of MXene@CDs is easily formed a protective film during the run-in period, which not only reduces the contact area of friction couples but also eliminates the polishing effect on the substrate surface, thus enabling synergistic lubrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Catechol groups functionalization
Carbon dots
Ti3C2Tx MXene
Synergistic lubrication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of catechol groups functionalized CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of CDs generated by pulsed laser synthesis. TEM images of (b) MXene, (c) CDs-2, (d) CDs-5, and (e) CDs-10 (insets: selective diffraction and size distribution of CDs). (f) HR-TEM image of CDs-5 (Mie-scattering image of CDs colloidal solution). (g) XPS spectrum of C 1s peaks, (h) Raman spectra and (i) PL spectra of different samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) HR-TEM image of MXene@CDs-5 (insets: low magnification TEM image of MXene@CDs-5). (b, c) FFT images and the line scan of the corresponding dotted regions. (d) HAADF image and (e–g) corresponding element mappings of MXene@CDs-5. (h) The bonding process of MXene and catechol group. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Raman spectra, (b) XRD pattern, (c) Zeta potentia, (d) TGA curve and XPS spectrum of (e) Ti 2p and (f) C 1s peaks of MXene, MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) COF curves of MXene@CDs-5 as PAO additives with a concentration of 0.05–2.0 wt%. (b) COF curves of MXene, MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 as additives (0.8 wt%). COF curves of the as-prepared samples under different conditions: (c) extreme pressure test (50 °C, 25 Hz) and (d) variable temperature test (150 N, 25 Hz). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The 3D images of worn surface lubricated by (a) PAO, (b) MXene, (c) MXene@CDs-2, (d) MXene@CDs-5 and (e) MXene@CDs-10. (f) The wear depth and (g) wear volumes of all samples. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SEM images of worn surface lubricated by (a) PAO, (b) MXene, (c) MXene@CDs-2, (d) MXene@CDs-5 and (e) MXene@CDs-10. XPS spectras of (f) Fe 2p, (g) Ti 2p, (h) C 1 s and (i) O 1s of worn surface lubricated by MXene@CDs-5 (150 N, 50 °C, 25 Hz). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Lamellar specimen preparation by the FIB-SEM, (b, c) TEM and HR-TEM images of the cross-section of wear scar, (d, e) HAADF image and the corresponding element mapping. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic illustration of CDs generated by pulsed laser treatment, and the optical images of TA solution before and after laser treatment (insert). (b) Schematic illustration of the preparation of MXene@CDs hybrids.
Lubrication mechanism of MXene@CDs as a nanoadditive.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of carbon dots intercalated MXene hybrids via laser treatment as oil-based additives for synergistic lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an, 710072, PR China

Center of Advanced Lubrication and Seal Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an emerging two-dimensional material, MXene exhibits excellent anti-friction and anti-wear properties in lubrication systems. However, as an oil-based lubricant additive, their stable dispersion in base oil still faces great challenges. Optimizing the microstructure has been proved to be an effective strategy to improve the stable dispersion of MXene in base oil. In this work, catechol groups functionalized carbon dots are synthesized successfully by pulsed laser irradiation of tannic acid in acetone, and then they are grafted onto MXene surface to obtain MXene@CDs hybrids via chemical bond between catechol groups of carbon dots and Ti–OH of MXene surface. Carbon dots not only increase the layer spacing of MXene nanosheets, reduce self-weight stacking, but also make it easier to slide between layers. When used as oil based lubricating additives, MXene@CDs can reduce the coefficient of friction of PAO 10 from 0.58 to 0.1, and the wear volume is reduced by 91.3%. Due to the shear-induced and self-healing mechanism, the special intercalation structure of MXene@CDs is easily formed a protective film during the run-in period, which not only reduces the contact area of friction couples but also eliminates the polishing effect on the substrate surface, thus enabling synergistic lubrication.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, energy issues and sustainable environmental development are gradually evolving into a global consensus. Friction and wear, as the most common phenomenon, have become one of the main factors causing energy consumption [1–3]. According to statistics, in modern industry, the energy consumed to overcome friction and wear is about 30%–40%. Lubrication plays an important role in improving the energy utilization of mechanical systems [4]. Unfortunately, conventional base oils do not have sufficient lubricity and durability to meet the requirements of stable mechanical operation. The introduction of nano-additives with anti-friction and anti-wear effects is a feasible method to improve the poor lubrication performance of base oil [5].

At present, two-dimensional (2D) materials as lubrication additives have been favored by researchers, the most common are graphene [6–8], black phosphorus [9], hexagonal boron nitride [10] and MoS2 [11]. This is because the layered structure and high specific surface area make it easy to slip between layers in the process of friction, giving full play to the interlayer shear mechanism and forming a stable friction protective film between friction pairs [9,11]. As an emerging layered 2D material, MXene has attracted a lot of attention since its discovery in 2011 [12]. It is mainly obtained by etching the middle metal layer of MAX. The chemical formula is Mn+1XnTx, where n = 1, 2, 3, M represents the transition metal element (i.e. Ti, V, and Mo), X is the carbon/nitrogen element, and Tx is the terminal functional group [13,14]. Monolayer MXene nanosheets consist of three, five or seven atomic layers and have larger layer spacing than graphene [15]. Due to the abundant functional groups (-OH, –F, =O) on the surface of MXene, it is easy to meet the required performance by surface modification and complexing with other materials compared to most 2D materials [16]. In terms of tribology, MXene is easy to form wear-resistant friction layer due to its low shear strength, in addition, the adhesive ability of MXene on the various surfaces passivates the contacts, significantly decreasing interfacial adhesion [17–19]. Therefore, MXene has attracted extensive attention on lubricating oil additives and solid lubricating coatings. For example, Grützmacher et al. [20] deposited a 100 nm thick coating of Ti3C2Tx MXene on stainless steel as a solid lubricant, and its wear life was at least 200% longer than that of the most advanced 2D materials in 105 sliding cycles. Similarly, the reports of MXene as a nano-additive have gradually increased in recent years. Although MXene has great potential in tribology, it has poor dispersion stability in non-polar solvent (base oil) as a lubricating additive due to a large number of hydrophilic groups on its surface. The most effective way is to functionalize it and optimize its microstructure. The most common is grafting some polar groups and long alkyl chain containing chemical reagents, such as dialkyl dithiophosphate (DDP) and tetradecylphosphonic acid (TDPA) [21]. Gao et al. [22] prepared DDP functionalized MXene nanosheets through Michael addition, the introduction of DDP significantly improved the dispersion stability of MXene nanosheets in base oil and showed good anti-friction and anti-wear performance. However, the introduction of organic modifiers may destroy the inherent structure of MXene and deteriorate its natural properties [21].

As a new member of the carbon material family, carbon dots (CDs) is widely used in biological imaging [23,24], catalysis [25], sensing [26] and energy storage [27,28] due to their good photostability, low toxicity and biocompatibility. CDs is composed of the central carbon nucleus and surface functional groups, with a size of less than 10 nm. Due to the different carbon sources, the functional groups carried on the CDs surface are also different, such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2. The rich edge sites of CDs enable them to be easily modified on the surface of substrate. In addition, the spherical structure of CDs can be used as a rolling bearing to convert sliding friction into rolling friction. At the same time, the adjustable surface groups also make it a promising lubricating material [29,30]. Tu et al. [31] prepared nickel doped carbon quantum dots (Ni-CQDs) by simple hydrothermal reaction, when the Ni-CQDs were added to polyethylene glycol (PEG-200) as additives, the average COF and wear rate of PEG-200 decreased by 35.5% and 36.4% respectively.

Herein, considering the synergistic effect of sliding friction and rolling friction, in this work, the catechol groups functionalized carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized successfully by pulsed laser irradiation using tannic acid (TA) as the raw material. After that, the as-obtained CDs can in-situ assemble on MXene to obtain MXene@CDs hybrids via bridge bidentate bonding of catechol groups with Ti–OH. The existence of catechol groups functionalized CDs not only increases the layer spacing of MXene, but also forms a barrier on MXene to avoid its self-weight stacking. The as-prepared MXene@CDs showed good stable dispersion in PAO 10. The tribological properties of MXene@CDs as lubricant additives in PAO 10 were experimentally investigated by a ball-on-disc tribometer and the lubrication mechanism was analyzed in details. This work provides a reference for the preparation of MXene based composites and enriches the research on MXene as lubricant additive.

Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), Tannic acid (TA) and Acetone were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. MAX (Ti3AlC2) was provided by Jinzhou Haixin Metal Materials Co., Ltd. Lithium fluoride (LiF) was obtained from Energy Chemical. The base oil PAO-10 came from China Oil Lanzhou Petrochemical Limited, and its viscosity was 118.96 mPa s at 25 °C when the rotational speed was 300 r/min. All chemical reagents were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of catechol groups functionalized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with catechol groups were synthesized by pulsed laser irradiation [32]. A Nd: YAG laser (pulse width 10 ns, repetition rate 10 Hz, beam size 10 mm) with a wavelength of 355 nm was used as the laser source for pulsed laser irradiation. First, 20, 50 and 100 mg of TA were dissolved in 10 mL of acetone by sonication. After that, the mixture was irradiated with an unfocused pulse beam of 828 mJ pulse−1 cm−2 (355 nm) for 8 min in an inert gas environment at room temperature, and the solution changed from transparent to yellow. The products with concentrations of 2, 5 and 10 mg/mL were denoted as CDs-2, CDs-5 and CDs-10, respectively.

Monolayer or few-layer MXene nanosheets were prepared according to the previous report [33]. First, 1 g of LiF was added to 20 mL (9 M) HCl under magnetic stirring for 5 min until it was completely dissolved. Then 1 g of MAX phase was added to the above mixture, and reacted at 35 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the mixture was washed with copious deionized water using centrifugation at 4000, 8000 and 10000 r/min successively for many times until the upper solution became viscous and dark green. After that, the upper layer solution was ultrasonically peeled off for 30 min. Finally, the stripped product was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 2 min. Removed the sediments not etched in the lower layer and collected the products in the upper layer.

10 mL of the above as-prepared MXene dispersion (8 mg/mL) were placed in three beakers. Then added 10 mL of CDs-2, 4 mL of CDs-5 and 2 mL of CDs-10 to the three beakers under stirring and the mixed solution were stirred for 12 h. The catechol groups of CDs surface can forme bidentate bonds with Ti of MXene via self-assembly method. Finally, the MXene@CDs were obtained by suction filtration and freeze drying. The products of MXene compounded with CDs-2, CDs-5 and CDs-10 were marked as MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10.

The morphology of raw materials and MXene@CDs were observed by double Cs Corrector Transmission Electron Microscope (Themis Z) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200X). Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension Icon) was used to measure the thickness of MXene nanosheets and CDs. The crystal structures of MXene and MXene@CDs were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE). The acceleration voltage was 40 kV, the tube current was 30 mA, and the diffraction angle scanning range was 2θ = 5–90°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, PHI 5000 VersaProbe III) were used to analyze the surface composition of materials and scratch of the steel block. The full spectrum range was 0–1100 eV, the scanning cycle was two times, the pass energy was 280 eV, and the step size was 1 eV. For the narrow spectrum, the elements were scanned 5 times and cycled twice, so the pass energy was 140 eV and the step size was 0.125 eV. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA 449F3) was carried out under Ar atmosphere (35–700 °C, 10 °C/min). The surface electrical property of the as-perpared materials were determined by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano. Raman spectroscopy (WITec Alpha300R) was used to study the composition of materials and the degree of carbonization of CDs. The optical absorbance was measured using the ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (LAMBDA 365). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were examined using a PL spectrometer (FLS 980, Edinburgh) with an excitation laser of 365 nm. The worn surface was crosscut by focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (FIB-SEM, FEI Strata 400S), and then the composition of the friction film was analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Talos F200x).

The tribological properties of the as-perpared samples were analyzed by SRV-5 tribometer on steel/steel contacts. The ball of the ball-on-disc mode was made of AISI 52100 steel (ø 10 mm, hardness: 60 ± 2 HRC, mean roughness: 20 nm), and the standard disc was made of the same steel (ø 24 mm × 7.9 mm, hardness: 62 ± 2 HRC). First, the lower disc was polished with water abrasive paper and polished with metallographic polishing cloth at 500 rpm. After that, the polished disc and steel ball were cleaned by ultrasonic wave. The disc was fixed on the table, kept absolutely still. The ball was fixed with a clamp, and the performance of the lubricating oil was tested by high-frequency reciprocating motion. Extreme pressure test: temperature 50 °C, frequency 25 Hz, amplitude 1 mm, load from 50 N to 800 N. Variable temperature test: load 150 N, frequency 25 Hz, amplitude 1 mm, temperature rose from 50 °C to 120 °C. The wear volume loss of the disk was tested on a three-dimensional (3D) surface profiler by Bruker.

Scheme 1 a described the process of preparing CDs by pulsed laser. Due to the extreme and non-equilibrium conditions required, this method had a strong attraction for the preparation of metastable nanomaterials [34]. The whole chain benzene ring of TA molecule was reconstituted into a graphite CDs with catechol groups on the surface by pulsed laser irradiation. The monolayer or few-layer MXene nanosheets were prepared by selective etching of Al layer in the middle of MAX phase by concentrated HCl and LiF. From the local selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image, it can be seen that the hexagonal crystal system structure of MXene nanosheets was well maintained (Fig. 1 b) [35]. Fig. S1a showed that the thickness of MXene nanosheets was about 3 nm. In addition, during the stripping process, a large number of groups (-OH, –F, =O) were introduced to the surface of MXene. As an active site, the surface Ti–OH can chemically bonded with the catechol group of the CDs, thus anchoring the CDs on the MXene surface (Scheme 1b).

The transient alternating heating and quenching generated by pulsed laser can successively make the whole chain benzene ring of TA converted into CDs through polymerization, carbonization, and graphitization, leaving catechol groups on the surface (Fig. 1a) [36]. The morphology of the as-prepared CDs via different concentrations of TA was characterized by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 1c–f and Fig. S1, the laser-generated CDs had a spherical structure, and the average particle sizes of CDs-2, CDs-5 and CDs-10 increased slightly with the TA concentration increasing. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) in Fig. 1f showed lattice stripes with a size of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (103) crystal plane of graphite carbon [37]. In addition, Tyndall scattering phenomenon can be seen in the yellow solution, indicating the successful generation of colloidal particles (bottom left inset of Fig. 1f). The surface structures of TA and CDs were analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1g and S2). It was clearly can be seen that the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of all samples showed three characteristic peaks (C–C, C–OH and C=O) and π-π* vibration satellite peak. After the carbon skeleton was reconstructed by benzene ring in TA, the phenolic hydroxyl groups were left on the surface of CDs, but the signals of the C=O peak and π-π* vibrational satellite peak were significantly weaker [38]. In addition, with TA concentration increasing, the C=O peak and C–OH peak shifted significantly, and the peak area gradually increased. This showed that the higher the concentration of TA, the more catechol groups carried on the surface of CDs. The Raman spectra of all CDs showed typical characteristic peaks at 1366 and 1588 cm−1, corresponding to the D band and G band of graphite carbon, respectively (Fig. 1h). In general, the D band represented the disorder or structural defect degree of sp2 hybrid carbon atoms or amorphous carbon deposits, while the G band was related to the plane tensile vibration of sp2 atoms in 2D hexagonal graphite lattice [39]. The value of ID/IG was used to evaluate the degree of graphitization of the sample. As shown in Fig. 1h, the ID/IG values of CDs-2, CDs-5 and CDs-10 were 0.71, 0.69 and 0.72, respectively, which have little difference and good graphitization degree. This was because the pulse laser power was the same, resulting in the same defect degree and graphitization degree of the as-obtained CDs. In addition, the UV–vis and PL spectra of the CDs solution were studied at room temperature (Fig. S3 and Fig. 1i). Compared with the original TA solution, CDs showed light absorption. When irradiated with 365 nm ultraviolet light, CDs-5 emitted blue fluorescence, indicating that the as-prepared CDs had a good fluorescence performance.

Fig. 2 a was the HR-TEM image of MXene@CDs-5 hybrids. As can be seen from the low magnification TEM image in the inset, the composite samples still maintained the lamellar skeleton structure, and the attached CDs can be seen on the ultrathin MXene nanosheet. In addition, HR-TEM and fast fourier transform (FFT) images clearly exbitted two distinct sets of lattice fringes with a crystal plane spacing of 0.269 nm and 0.21 nm (Fig. 2b and c), corresponded to the (100) crystal planes of MXene and (103) crystal planes of graphite carbon [40]. Element mapping analysis proved the uniform distribution of O, C and Ti elements in MXene@CDs-5 (Fig. 2d–g). Fig. 2h was the combination process of MXene and CDs. A large number of oxygen containing groups such as –OH, =O were introduced on the etched MXene surface, so the Ti–OH groups acted as the active site to bind the catechol group, anchoring the CDs to the MXene nanosheets.

In order to further prove the successful recombination of MXene and CDs, the graphitization degree of the as-prepared samples was measured by Raman spectra. Compared with MXene, MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 all showed two distinct graphite-carbon peaks in D band and G band (Fig. 3 a). These carbon peaks were caused by the loading of CDs, which proved that CDs were successfully loaded onto MXene nanosheets. At the same time, the two additional peaks at 396 and 617 cm−1 may be caused by the vibration of anatase [41]. In addition, the peak of Eg mode caused by the external vibration of the anatase TiO2 was easily observed (151 cm−1), which indicated that the oxidation degree of MXene@CDs was higher than MXene. This was because the recombination was carried out in a room temperature and water environment, a small amount of Ti on the MXene surface was oxidized to TiO2 during the reaction. The XRD pattern in Fig. 3b showed that compared with MXene, due to the interlayer expansion, the (002) crystal face of the MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 had a lower diffraction peak angle shift. Moreover, as the size of CDs-2, CDs-5 and CDs-10 gradually increased, the offset angle became larger. It also confirmed that the bond combination of CDs did not damage the layer structure of MXene. Because of the large number of groups (-OH, –F, =O) carried on the surface of MXene, the zeta potential of them is −31.30 mV. After recombination with CDs, the surface had more negative charge, which may be due to the large amount of catechol groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. 3c). The thermal stability of the as-prepared samples was analyzed in Ar atmosphere, and shown in Fig. 3d. Due to the adsorbed water and residual HF on the surface, the TGA curves of all samples showed weight loss in the first stage (below 300 °C). The weight loss after 300 °C was due to the removal of functional groups in the samples. In addition, the weight loss rate of MXene@CDs increased, and the calcined residues decreased significantly. The surface chemical state of MXene@CDs was determined by XPS (Fig. S4). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ti 2p was shown in Fig. 3e. MXene had four characteristic peaks at 457.5, 458.8, 460.7 and 464.1 eV, which was corresponded to Ti–C, Ti 2p3/2, Ti–O and Ti 2p1/2, respectively. Compared with MXene, the areas of the Ti–O peaks of MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 were significantly smaller, and the central positions of the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti–C peaks were shifted. This was because the binding energy of the MXene atomic orbital was changed due to the bonding with catechol group of CDs. The C 1s spectrum showed four fitted peaks (C–Ti, C–C, C–OH and C=O). In the O 1s spectrum, the characteristic peaks at 528.3, 530.6 and 531.93 eV were attributed to Lattice O, Ti–O and C–Ti-(OH)x, respectively (Fig. S4b) [42,43].

The dispersion stability of nano-additives in base oil was the key to the tribological performance of lubricant [44]. First, Ti3C2Tx MXene, MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5, and MXene@Cu-10 were fully dispersed in the base oil PAO 10 by sonication to obtain a stable black mixture. The concentration of all mixtures was 0.8 wt %. After that, the samples were left to stand to observe the precipitation state (Fig. S5). When the samples were left to stand for two days, a small amount of MXene precipitated in PAO 10, while MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 still showed stable dispersion, which may be due to the large amount of catechol groups on the CDs surface. After standing for five days, the precipitation of MXene became more pronounced, while a small amount of precipitation started to appear in MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10.

In order to determine the test concentration, we have investigated the tribological properties of MXene@CDs-5 with the concentration of 0.05–0.2 wt% (load: 150 N, frequency: 25 Hz, temperature: 50 °C, stroke: 1 mm). As shown in Fig. 4 a, in the initial stage, the coefficient of friction (COF) of PAO 10 rapidly increased to 0.58, and then decreased to a relatively stable value, hovering around 0.16. This was because under the initial operating conditions, the high contact pressure of point contacts caused the oil film to break, leading to lubrication failure, which was consistent with the previous report [45]. When the additive concentration was 0.05 wt%, because the low additive concentration cannot form a continuous and stable protective film on the friction pair surface, the same seizure failure also occurred. With the further increase of the added concentration, the COF gradually decreased. However when the concentration exceeded 0.8 wt%, the COF started to increase, which indicated that when the additive concentration was too large, excess MXene@CDs-5 will aggregate at the interface, leading to the destruction of membrane continuity [46]. Consistent with the anti-friction results, the samples with 0.8 wt% added in Fig. S6 and Fig. S7 also showed excellent anti-wear performance. Therefore, under the same test conditions, the tribological properties of the as-prepared samples were evaluated when the additive concentration was 0.8 wt%. As shown in Fig. 4b, after adding MXene nanosheets, although there was a running in period at the beginning, the initial friction peak was avoided, and the average COF of PAO 10 decreased from 0.135 to 0.119 (Fig. S8). After the addition of MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10, the COF curves over time were close to a straight line, and the average COF decreased to 0.111, 0.103 and 0.100, respectively, indicating that the existence of CDs provided better lubrication conditions. This was because the intercalation structure of MXene@CDs can form a stable MXene protective film and carbon film during the process of friction, thus hindering the direct contact between friction pairs. In addition, we tested the COF curves for different samples under extreme environmental conditions. The loading capacity of base oil PAO 10 was 250 N (25 Hz, 50 °C), and the loading capacity of MXene based lubricant was slightly increased, with the first seizure occurring at 300 N and severe seizure occurring at 350 N. When MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 were added, the loading capacity was increased to 350 N, 650 N and 400 N, respectively (Fig. 4c). MXene@CDs-5 showed the best loading capacity among all the samples, indicating that the size and catechol groups of CDs had important effects on the tribological properties of MXene@CDs hybrids. Variable temperature test was shown in Fig. 4d, the COF of PAO 10 was relatively stable at a lower temperature (below 60 °C). When the temperature exceeded 60 °C, the COF increased to 0.19. After adding MXene, the failure temperature of PAO 10 was increased to 80 °C. The CDs further suppressed the lubrication failure of PAO 10, and the COF was close to a straight line when the temperature varied from 40 to 120 °C.

In order to further evaluate the anti-wear properties of different samples, the surface morphology of the scratches on the lower disk of the friction pair was observed by a three-dimensional (3D) profiler. As shown in Fig. 5 , the worn surface lubricated by PAO 10 was very rough, and we can clearly see wide and deep grooves on the surface. The wear depth of MXene based lubricant decreased from −2.709 μm to −0.634 μm, and their wear volume decreased by 76.9%, indicating that MXene nanosheets were deposited on the steel plate and formed a protective film. Compared with PAO 10 and MXene, the scratches and grooves lubricated by MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10 were shallower, and the wear depth decreased to −0.500, −0.499 and −0.474 μm, the wear volume decreased by 88.7%, 91.1% and 91.3%, respectively. This showed that the synergistic effect of MXene and CDs can make it have excellent anti-wear performance.

Fig. 6 a–e showed the SEM images of worn surface lubricated by the as-prepared samples. It can be seen that there were deep furrows and obvious pits on the worn surface of PAO 10, indicating that the friction pair had suffered serious wear. This was because in the process of friction, the oil film was destroyed due to the existence of pressure. The direct contact of friction pairs led to serious wear and the formation of a large amount of abrasive debris, which further caused abrasive wear between the friction pairs and aggravated the wear [47,48]. After addition of MXene, the pits on the surface became smaller, and tiny wear debris can be seen, indicating that MXene can also form a protective film during friction, preventing direct contact between friction pairs. After lubricated by MXene@CDs-2, MXene@CDs-5 and MXene@CDs-10, the worn surface became smooth with only very shallow embossed grain, and the pits disappeared, which showed that the introduction of CDs further improved the wear resistance of MXene. In order to explore the lubrication mechanism, the tribological reaction and the friction film formed on the wear interface were analyzed by XPS (Fig. S9). As shown in Fig. 6f, Fe 2p3/2 fitted two characteristic peaks at 710.7 and 712.8 eV, corresponding to Fe3O4 and Fe2O3. The peaks of Ti 2p spectra at 457.9, 459.6, 463.1 and 464.2 eV were attributed to Ti–O, Ti2+, Ti 2p1/2 and TiO2-xFx (Fig. 6g). The characteristic peaks of C 1s spectrum at 284.9, 286.0 and 288.7 eV in Fig. 6h were caused by C–C, C–OH and C=O. The peaks of Ti–O and iron oxides in O 1s spectra corresponded to those in Fe 2p and Ti 2p (Fig. 6i) [49]. This result indicated that in the process of friction, the local high temperature of the contact surface caused the tribochemical reaction between oxygen in the air and metal, and generated the tribochemical reaction film composed of Fe3O4, Fe2O3 and TiO2 [50]. In addition, under high load, MXene and CDs were adsorbed on the wear interface, forming MXene adsorption film and carbon film. The existence of these protective films was a key factor to reduce friction and wear.

In order to further analyze the friction protective film, the cross-sectional layer of the wear area was observed by focused ion beam technique-TEM (FIB-TEM). Fig. 7 a showed the SEM image of the cross section, and then TEM was used to analyze the friction protective film interface in the white frame line. From Fig. 7b and c, we can see three different interfaces. The upper layer was the Pt/C protective layer, the lower layer was the steel block matrix, and the bright part in the middle was the friction protective film. It can be seen that the protective film left on the sliding interface was dense and uniform in thickness, about 20 nm. At the same time, the element mapping analysis intuitively showed the element composition of the friction film (Fig. 7e). A strong Ti signal can be observed in the middle region, indicating that the friction film was mainly composed of MXene and MXene@CDs. In addition, Fe and O elements can be seen near the steel plate, indicating that the tribochemical reaction occurred on the surface of the steel matrix, and metal oxides appeared.

Based on the above analysis, the reasons for anti-friction and anti-wear of MXene@CDs composite materials can be summarized as follows (Scheme 2 ). Firstly, the good dispersion stability of MXene@CDs in PAO 10 ensured that it can continuously enter the contact area of the friction pair, which was the primary condition for it to fully exert its lubrication mechanism [51]. Secondly, at high contact pressure, MXene@CDs broke into smaller fragments, which can spontaneously embed into the grooves of the worn area and repair the friction interface, thus reducing the roughness. In addition, CDs expanded the layer spacing of MXene, making it easier to slip between layers, and giving full play to the interlayer shear mechanism. At the same time, the negatively charged MXene@CDs was easily adsorbed on the surface of the friction pair, forming a continuous and stable MXene adsorption film and carbon film. Finally, the high temperature generated by frictional sliding promoted the rapid tribochemical reaction between metal and oxygen, resulting in the formation of a tribochemical reaction film composed of Fe3O4, Fe2O3 and TiO2, which prevented the direct contact between friction pairs. Therefore, the excellent tribological properties of MXene@CDs were not only related to physical adsorption and deposition, but also depended on the complex tribochemical reactions.

In this paper, the catechol groups functionalized carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized successfully by pulsed laser irradiation of TA solution, and then they can in-situ assemble on MXene to obtain MXene@CDs hybrids via bridge bidentate bonding of catechol groups with Ti–OH on MXene surface. The intercalation structure of MXene@CDs not only expanded the interlayer spacing of MXene, but also reduced its self-weight stacking. Due to its special microstructure, MXene@CDs exhibited excellent tribological properties. When 0.8 wt% MXene@CDs was added to PAO 10, the COF decreased from 0.58 to 0.1 and the wear volume decreased by 91.3%, the carrying capacity can reach 650 N. This was mainly due to the special interlayer structure of MXene@CDs formed a protective film during the process of friction, the protective films not only reduced the sliding contact area, but also eliminated the polishing effect of the substrate surface, thus achieving synergistic lubrication.

Yuhong Cui: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Shenghua Xue: Investigation. Shiyuan Wang: Data curation. Xin Chen: Data curation. Shujuan Liu: Writing – review & editing. Qian Ye: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Feng Zhou: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Weimin Liu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A2046), the Western Light Project of CAS (xbzg-zdsys-202118) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.
